content:
# Genesis Chapter 11 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 6v-7r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_011_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_011_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 7-8 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

*The Tower of Babel (vv. 1–9)*

**11:1** And all the earth [וַיְהִי כָל-הָאָרֶץ] was one lip [שָׂפָה אֶחָת] and one words [וּדְבָרִים אֲחָדִים].

**11:2** And it was [וַיְהִי] as they journeyed from the east [בְּנָסְעָם מִקֶּדֶם] that they found a plain [וַיִּמְצְאוּ בִקְעָה] in the land of Shinar [בְּאֶרֶץ שִׁנְעָר] and they settled there [וַיֵּשְׁבוּ שָׁם].

**11:3** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ] each man to his neighbor [אִישׁ אֶל-רֵעֵהוּ]: Come [הָבָה], let us make bricks [נִלְבְּנָה לְבֵנִים] and burn them to a burning [וְנִשְׂרְפָה לִשְׂרֵפָה]. And the brick was to them [וַתְּהִי לָהֶם הַלְּבֵנָה] for stone [לְאָבֶן] and the bitumen [וְהַחֵמָר] was to them for mortar [הָיָה לָהֶם לַחֹמֶר].

**11:4** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: Come [הָבָה], let us build for ourselves [נִבְנֶה-לָּנוּ] a city and a tower [עִיר וּמִגְדָּל] with its top in the heavens [וְרֹאשׁוֹ בַשָּׁמַיִם] and let us make for ourselves a name [וְנַעֲשֶׂה-לָּנוּ שֵׁם] lest we be scattered [פֶּן-נָפוּץ] over the face of all the earth [עַל-פְּנֵי כָל-הָאָרֶץ].

**11:5** And the LORD came down [וַיֵּרֶד יְהוָה] to see [לִרְאֹת] the city and the tower [אֶת-הָעִיר וְאֶת-הַמִּגְדָּל] which the sons of man had built [אֲשֶׁר בָּנוּ בְּנֵי הָאָדָם].

**11:6** And the LORD said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה]: Behold [הֵן], they are one people [עַם אֶחָד] and one lip is to all of them [וְשָׂפָה אַחַת לְכֻלָּם] and this is what they have begun to do [וְזֶה הַחִלָּם לַעֲשׂוֹת]; and now [וְעַתָּה] nothing will be withheld from them [לֹא-יִבָּצֵר מֵהֶם] of all that they plan to do [כֹּל אֲשֶׁר יָזְמוּ לַעֲשׂוֹת].

**11:7** Come [הָבָה], let us go down [נֵרְדָה] and confuse there [וְנָבְלָה שָׁם] their lip [שְׂפָתָם] so that a man will not hear [אֲשֶׁר לֹא יִשְׁמְעוּ] the lip of his neighbor [אִישׁ שְׂפַת רֵעֵהוּ].

**11:8** And the LORD scattered them [וַיָּפֶץ יְהוָה אֹתָם] from there [מִשָּׁם] over the face of all the earth [עַל-פְּנֵי כָל-הָאָרֶץ] and they ceased [וַיַּחְדְּלוּ] from building the city [מִבְּנֹת הָעִיר].

**11:9** Therefore [עַל-כֵּן] its name was called [קָרָא שְׁמָהּ] Babel [בָּבֶל] because there [כִּי-שָׁם] the LORD confused [בָּלַל יְהוָה] the lip of all the earth [שְׂפַת כָּל-הָאָרֶץ] and from there [וּמִשָּׁם] the LORD scattered them [הֱפִיצָם יְהוָה] over the face of all the earth [עַל-פְּנֵי כָל-הָאָרֶץ].

---

*The Generations of Shem (vv. 10–26)*

**11:10** These are the generations [אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת] of Shem [שֵׁם]: Shem was one hundred years old [שֵׁם בֶּן-מְאַת שָׁנָה] and fathered [וַיּוֹלֶד] Arphaxad [אֶת-אַרְפַּכְשַׁד] two years after the flood [שְׁנָתַיִם אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל].

**11:11** And Shem lived [וַיְחִי-שֵׁם] after his fathering Arphaxad [אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת-אַרְפַּכְשַׁד] five hundred years [חֲמֵשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה] and fathered sons and daughters [וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת].

**11:12** And Arphaxad lived [וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁד חַי] thirty-five years [חָמֵשׁ וּשְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה] and fathered [וַיּוֹלֶד] Shelah [אֶת-שָׁלַח].

**11:13** And Arphaxad lived [וַיְחִי אַרְפַּכְשַׁד] after his fathering Shelah [אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת-שָׁלַח] four hundred and three years [שָׁלֹשׁ שָׁנִים וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה] and fathered sons and daughters [וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת].

**11:14** And Shelah lived [וְשֶׁלַח חַי] thirty years [שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה] and fathered [וַיּוֹלֶד] Eber [אֶת-עֵבֶר].

**11:15** And Shelah lived [וַיְחִי שֶׁלַח] after his fathering Eber [אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת-עֵבֶר] four hundred and three years [שָׁלֹשׁ שָׁנִים וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה] and fathered sons and daughters [וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת].

**11:16** And Eber lived [וַיְחִי-עֵבֶר] thirty-four years [אַרְבַּע וּשְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה] and fathered [וַיּוֹלֶד] Peleg [אֶת-פֶּלֶג].

**11:17** And Eber lived [וַיְחִי עֵבֶר] after his fathering Peleg [אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת-פֶּלֶג] four hundred and thirty years [שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה] and fathered sons and daughters [וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת].

**11:18** And Peleg lived [וַיְחִי-פֶלֶג] thirty years [שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה] and fathered [וַיּוֹלֶד] Reu [אֶת-רְעוּ].

**11:19** And Peleg lived [וַיְחִי פֶלֶג] after his fathering Reu [אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת-רְעוּ] two hundred and nine years [תֵּשַׁע שָׁנִים וּמָאתַיִם שָׁנָה] and fathered sons and daughters [וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת].

**11:20** And Reu lived [וַיְחִי-רְעוּ] thirty-two years [שְׁתַּיִם וּשְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה] and fathered [וַיּוֹלֶד] Serug [אֶת-שְׂרוּג].

**11:21** And Reu lived [וַיְחִי רְעוּ] after his fathering Serug [אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת-שְׂרוּג] two hundred and seven years [שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים וּמָאתַיִם שָׁנָה] and fathered sons and daughters [וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת].

**11:22** And Serug lived [וַיְחִי-שְׂרוּג] thirty years [שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה] and fathered [וַיּוֹלֶד] Nahor [אֶת-נָחוֹר].

**11:23** And Serug lived [וַיְחִי שְׂרוּג] after his fathering Nahor [אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת-נָחוֹר] two hundred years [מָאתַיִם שָׁנָה] and fathered sons and daughters [וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת].

**11:24** And Nahor lived [וַיְחִי-נָחוֹר] twenty-nine years [תֵּשַׁע וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה] and fathered [וַיּוֹלֶד] Terah [אֶת-תֶּרַח].

**11:25** And Nahor lived [וַיְחִי נָחוֹר] after his fathering Terah [אַחֲרֵי הוֹלִידוֹ אֶת-תֶּרַח] one hundred and nineteen years [תֵּשַׁע עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה וּמְאַת שָׁנָה] and fathered sons and daughters [וַיּוֹלֶד בָּנִים וּבָנוֹת].

**11:26** And Terah lived [וַיְחִי-תֶרַח] seventy years [שִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה] and fathered [וַיּוֹלֶד] Abram [אֶת-אַבְרָם] and Nahor [אֶת-נָחוֹר] and Haran [וְאֶת-הָרָן].

---

*The Generations of Terah (vv. 27–32)*

**11:27** And these are the generations [וְאֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת] of Terah [תֶּרַח]: Terah fathered [תֶּרַח הוֹלִיד] Abram [אֶת-אַבְרָם] and Nahor [אֶת-נָחוֹר] and Haran [וְאֶת-הָרָן]; and Haran fathered [וְהָרָן יָלַד] Lot [אֶת-לוֹט].

**11:28** And Haran died [וַיָּמָת הָרָן] upon the face of [עַל-פְּנֵי] his father Terah [אָבִיו תֶּרַח] in the land of his birth [בְּאֶרֶץ מוֹלַדְתּוֹ] in Ur of the Chaldeans [בְּאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים].

**11:29** And Abram and Nahor took [וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָם וְנָחוֹר לָהֶם] wives [נָשִׁים]: the name of Abram's wife [שֵׁם אֵשֶׁת-אַבְרָם] was Sarai [שָׂרָי] and the name of Nahor's wife [וְשֵׁם אֵשֶׁת-נָחוֹר] was Milcah [מִלְכָּה] the daughter of Haran [בַּת-הָרָן] the father of Milcah [אֲבִי-מִלְכָּה] and the father of Iscah [וַאֲבִי יִסְכָּה].

**11:30** And Sarai was barren [וַתְּהִי שָׂרַי עֲקָרָה] — she had no child [אֵין לָהּ וָלָד].

**11:31** And Terah took [וַיִּקַּח תֶּרַח] Abram his son [אֶת-אַבְרָם בְּנוֹ] and Lot son of Haran [וְאֶת-לוֹט בֶּן-הָרָן] son of his son [בֶּן-בְּנוֹ] and Sarai his daughter-in-law [וְאֵת שָׂרַי כַּלָּתוֹ] wife of Abram his son [אֵשֶׁת אַבְרָם בְּנוֹ] and they went out with them [וַיֵּצְאוּ אִתָּם] from Ur of the Chaldeans [מֵאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים] to go to the land of Canaan [לָלֶכֶת אַרְצָה כְּנָעַן] and they came [וַיָּבֹאוּ] as far as Haran [עַד-חָרָן] and they settled there [וַיֵּשְׁבוּ שָׁם].

**11:32** And the days of Terah were [וַיִּהְיוּ יְמֵי-תֶרַח] two hundred and five years [חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים וּמָאתַיִם שָׁנָה] and Terah died [וַיָּמָת תֶּרַח] in Haran [בְּחָרָן].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 11:1 — שָׂפָה אֶחָת וּדְבָרִים אֲחָדִים: "One Lip and One Words"

וַיְהִי כָל-הָאָרֶץ שָׂפָה אֶחָת וּדְבָרִים אֲחָדִים — "And all the earth was one lip and one words."

This verse contains two deliberate anomalies that the EMET translation preserves in its literal rendering:

**שָׂפָה (safah, "lip")** rather than לָשׁוֹן (lashon, "tongue") or שָׂפָה in its common sense. The word שָׂפָה means literally "lip" — the physical instrument of speech. Its use here (and in vv. 7 and 9) rather than the more common "tongue" is a stylistic choice that: (1) emphasizes the physical, bodily act of speech, (2) creates an image of unified utterance at a most fundamental level — one lip, one mouth, one voice for all humanity, and (3) sets up the wordplay with בָּלַל (balal, "to confuse/mix") in v. 9, where the lip that was one becomes confused.

**דְּבָרִים אֲחָדִים (devarim achadim, "one words"):** This phrase is grammatically striking. The word אֲחָדִים is the plural of אֶחָד (one) — literally "ones" or "single [ones]." The phrase could mean: (a) a few words / a limited vocabulary, (b) the same words / unified speech, or (c) one set of words / a common vocabulary. The plural אֲחָדִים is also used in Genesis 27:44 (יָמִים אֲחָדִים, "a few days") and Song of Songs 1:13 (in a different construction). The sense here oscillates between "unified words" (one common language) and "a few/limited words" (a single, perhaps primitive tongue). The EMET preserves the strangeness: "one words" — the grammatical tension itself is a clue to the translator's intent.

**LXX 11:1:** καὶ ἦν πᾶσα ἡ γῆ χεῖλος ἕν καὶ φωνὴ μία πᾶσιν — "And all the earth was one lip and one voice for all." The LXX renders דְּבָרִים אֲחָדִים as φωνὴ μία (one voice) rather than "one words" — a smoothing of the difficult Hebrew into a more natural Greek idiom. The LXX adds πᾶσιν ("for all") which has no direct MT equivalent.

**The narrative function of v. 1:** The verse sets up the Babel story as the reversal of the post-flood dispersal described in Genesis 10. In Genesis 10 the nations are already spread "each according to his tongue" (10:5, 20, 31) — yet Genesis 11:1 opens by saying "all the earth was one lip." Scholars call this a classic "back-flash" technique: chapters 10 and 11 are not sequential but describe the same period from different angles (ch. 10 from above, as the result; ch. 11 from the ground, as the process). Or alternatively, Genesis 10 is the post-Babel outcome viewed proleptically, and Genesis 11:1-9 is the narrative that explains how that diversity came to be.

---

### On 11:2 — מִקֶּדֶם / From the East

בְּנָסְעָם מִקֶּדֶם — "as they journeyed from the east." The preposition מִקֶּדֶם is directional: "from the east." However, קֶדֶם can also mean "from of old / from ancient times" as well as "eastward." The LXX renders ἀπὸ ἀνατολῶν (from the east), supporting the directional reading. The Samaritan Pentateuch has a variant. Some interpreters read "eastward" (toward the east) rather than "from the east," which would reverse the direction of travel. The MT (Leningrad Codex) supports "from the east" — which, given the Mesopotamian location of Shinar, implies the migrants came from a more eastern point (Armenia? the mountains of Ararat where the ark landed?). Moving westward, they arrive in the Tigris-Euphrates plain (Shinar/Babylonia).

---

### On 11:3 — The Brick Technology: A Mesopotamian Detail

**וְנִשְׂרְפָה לִשְׂרֵפָה (wenisrepha lisrefah, "and burn them to a burning"):** The infinitive absolute construction emphasizes the completeness of the burning — fire-baked bricks, not merely sun-dried. This is a significant cultural marker: in the land of Canaan/Israel, stone was the primary building material. In Mesopotamia, where stone is scarce on the alluvial plain, fired brick was the standard construction material. The explanatory parenthetical in v. 3b — "the brick was to them for stone, and the bitumen was to them for mortar" — is the narrator's gloss for an Israelite audience unfamiliar with Mesopotamian building technology. חֵמָר (chemar, "bitumen/asphalt") was indeed used as mortar in ancient Mesopotamian construction (documented archaeologically at Ur and Uruk). The text is remarkably accurate about Mesopotamian construction.

---

### On 11:4 — The Tower and the Name

**מִגְדָּל (migdal, "tower"):** The word is used for a military watchtower (Judg. 8:9), a tall structure for defense or signaling. In the context of Babel/Babylon, the reference is most naturally to the Babylonian ziggurat — a stepped temple-tower whose summit was a shrine dedicated to the city's patron deity. The Babylonian ziggurat at Babylon was called Etemenanki — "House of the Foundation of Heaven and Earth" — and its rebuilding by Nebuchadnezzar II (c. 605-562 BCE) made it one of the most famous structures of the ancient world, possibly reaching 90 meters in height. The Hebrew phrase **וְרֹאשׁוֹ בַשָּׁמַיִם (verosho vashamayim, "with its top in the heavens")** corresponds exactly to the Babylonian self-description of Etemenanki and other ziggurats as structures that reach from earth to heaven.

**וְנַעֲשֶׂה-לָּנוּ שֵׁם (venaase-lanu shem, "and let us make for ourselves a name"):** This clause is the ideological heart of the Babel story. שֵׁם (shem, "name/renown") is both the common word for "name" and the name of Noah's son Shem (the ancestor of the Semites, the one from whose line Abram will come). The desire to "make a name" is the human drive toward self-perpetuating fame, self-sufficiency, self-deification — the assertion of the creature's permanence and significance apart from the Creator. The irony of the narrative is total: YHWH will instead make Abram's name great (12:2 — וַאֲגַדְּלָה שְׁמֶךָ, "I will make your name great") as a gift of grace, contrasting with the futile human attempt at self-aggrandizement at Babel.

**פֶּן-נָפוּץ (pen-nafuts, "lest we be scattered"):** The builders fear the scattering — yet scattering is precisely what YHWH's judgment produces (v. 8: וַיָּפֶץ יְהוָה, "and the LORD scattered them"). The same root (פוּץ, puts) is used in both the fear and the fulfillment: the builders' attempt to prevent scattering by gathering brings about the very scattering they dreaded.

---

### On 11:5 — וַיֵּרֶד יְהוָה: "The LORD Came Down"

This verse is one of the boldest anthropomorphisms in Genesis. The LORD "comes down" — a spatial movement from above to below — to see what the humans have built. The verb יָרַד (yarad, "to descend, come down") is used elsewhere for theophanic descents (Exod. 19:11, 18, 20; 34:5 — the Sinai theophany). The divine descent to "see" is emphatic: God is not absent or ignorant; he perceives the human project and acts in response to what he sees. There is a gentle irony in v. 5 — the tower whose top was meant to reach the heavens is so small that God must "come down" to even see it.

**LXX 11:5:** καὶ κατέβη κύριος ἰδεῖν τὴν πόλιν καὶ τὸν πύργον — "And the LORD came down to see the city and the tower." The LXX is in close agreement with the MT here.

---

### On 11:6 — The Logic of Divine Intervention

**עַם אֶחָד וְשָׂפָה אַחַת לְכֻלָּם (am echad vesafah achat lekulam, "one people and one lip is to all of them"):** God's speech (vv. 6-7) makes explicit what was implied in v. 1: the unity of language enables a unity of purpose and capability. The statement is not a condemnation of human intelligence or ambition per se, but an acknowledgment of the power of coordinated human action. YHWH's assessment — "nothing will be withheld from them of all that they plan to do" (לֹא-יִבָּצֵר מֵהֶם כֹּל אֲשֶׁר יָזְמוּ לַעֲשׂוֹת) — is an endorsement of human capability, not a dismissal of it. The concern is not that humanity will succeed at building a tall tower, but that unchecked and undispersed humanity will achieve things that, in God's economy, are not yet appropriate.

**וְזֶה הַחִלָּם לַעֲשׂוֹת (vezeh hachilam laasot, "and this is what they have begun to do"):** The root חָלַל (chalal, "to begin") is the same used for Nimrod (10:8: הֵחֵל לִהְיוֹת גִּבֹּר — "he began to be a mighty one"). The structural echo links Nimrod's city-building empire (10:10-12) to the Babel project: Babel is Nimrod's city, and the tower-building is the culmination of the post-flood human drive toward imperial unity.

---

### On 11:7 — נֵרְדָה: The Divine Plural "Let Us Go Down"

**הָבָה נֵרְדָה (havah nerdah, "Come, let us go down"):** The divine plural — first-person plural cohortative — appears three times in Genesis: 1:26 ("let us make man"), 3:22 ("the man has become like one of us"), and here 11:7 ("let us go down"). All three instances use plural address in divine speech in a context where no divine council is explicitly mentioned. Interpretations:

1. **The divine council (Heavenly Court):** YHWH addresses the divine council (cf. 1 Kgs. 22:19-22; Job 1:6; Ps. 82) — the angelic or divine beings of the heavenly court. This is the most common critical interpretation and has ancient support (Targum Onkelos reads it as plural but interposes the divine council; rabbinic interpretation notes the unusual usage).

2. **Plural of majesty / deliberative plural:** The "royal we" — the plural of self-address used by a monarch. Ancient Near Eastern kings address themselves in the plural; YHWH's plural may be a deliberative: "let me go down / let us [= I, royally] go down."

3. **Trinitarian interpretation:** Patristic Christian interpretation (e.g., Justin Martyr, Irenaeus, Tertullian) reads the plural as evidence of intra-divine plurality — Father, Son, and Spirit in deliberation. This reading reads Genesis 1:26, 3:22, and 11:7 as windows into the divine Trinity.

**EMET note:** The EMET translation renders the plural literally as "let us go down" — preserving the grammatical plurality without resolving the theological question. The same policy applies to 1:26 and 3:22.

---

### On 11:9 — בָּבֶל / בָּלַל: The Babel-Balal Wordplay

**כִּי-שָׁם בָּלַל יְהוָה שְׂפַת כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (ki-sham balal YHWH sefat kol-haaretz, "because there the LORD confused the lip of all the earth"):**

This is the most famous wordplay in Genesis 11 and one of the great folk etymologies of the Hebrew Bible. The name **בָּבֶל (Babel)** is explained by the verb **בָּלַל (balal, "to mix, confuse, mingle")**. The connection is phonetically clever: Babel → Balal. However, the actual etymology of בָּבֶל is Akkadian: **Bāb-ilim** ("Gate of God/Gods") — a name of honor for the great city. The Hebrew narrator is making a polemical counter-etymology: what the Babylonians called "The Gate of God" — their portal to heaven, the place where earth meets the divine — the Hebrews name "Confusion," the place where YHWH disrupted human pretension.

This is a theological act of naming: by re-etymologizing Babylon from "gate of god" to "confusion," the Hebrew tradition unmasks the imperial capital of the ancient world. Babylon, which called itself the cosmic center and the meeting place of heaven and earth, is in Hebrew understanding the place of confusion and scattering.

**The שָׂפָה (safah, "lip") wordplay:** The use of שָׂפָה throughout the narrative — "one lip" (v. 1), "confuse their lip" (v. 7), "the lip of all the earth" (v. 9) — is deliberate and unified. The "lip" that was one becomes many. The instrument of unified human communication becomes the site of divine intervention. בָּלַל as "to confuse a lip" is unusual (the more common object would be "language" or "speech") and emphasizes that it is the physical organ — the mouth, the capacity for shared utterance — that is disrupted at its most basic level.

**LXX 11:9:** καὶ διὰ τοῦτο ἐκλήθη τὸ ὄνομα αὐτῆς Σύγχυσις (Sygchysis), ὅτι ἐκεῖ συνέχεε κύριος τὰ χείλη πάσης τῆς γῆς — "And for this reason its name was called Confusion, because there the LORD mixed/confused the lips of all the earth." The LXX does not transliterate "Babel" but translates it as Σύγχυσις (Sygchysis, "Confusion/Mixing") — making the etymology explicit in Greek. The LXX uses the verb συγχέω (to pour together, to confuse) as its equivalent of בָּלַל.

---

### On 11:10 — The Fifth Toledot Formula and the Shem Genealogy

**אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת שֵׁם (elleh toledot Shem, "these are the generations of Shem"):** This is the fifth toledot formula in Genesis (2:4 heavens/earth; 5:1 Adam; 6:9 Noah; 10:1 sons of Noah; 11:10 Shem). The Shem toledot bridges from the universal Table of Nations to the particular line that will produce Abraham. It is the linchpin genealogy of the primeval history.

**The chronological pattern:** Each entry follows a rigid formula: (1) age at first son, (2) birth of named son, (3) remaining years, (4) "and he fathered sons and daughters." This formula is identical to Genesis 5 (the pre-flood patriarchs) but the ages are dramatically shorter — the post-flood patriarchs live significantly less long than their pre-flood counterparts. This is often noted as a gradual decline from the antediluvian lifespans (e.g., Methuselah: 969 years) through the post-flood patriarchs to Abraham (175 years) and eventually the normal human lifespan.

---

### On 11:10-26 — LXX Chronology: The Cainan Variant and Age Differences

This section contains the most significant textual difference between the MT and LXX in all of Genesis 11.

**The LXX Cainan insertion (LXX 11:12-13):**

The LXX inserts a complete generation — Cainan (Καϊναν) — between Arphaxad and Shelah:

| MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) |
|----------------------|-----------------------|
| Arphaxad fathered Shelah (v. 12) | Arphaxad fathered Cainan |
| [no Cainan entry] | Cainan lived 130 years and fathered Shelah |
| | Cainan lived 330 years after fathering Shelah |

This Cainan (distinct from the pre-flood Cainan/Kenan of Genesis 5:9-14 — same name, different person) adds approximately 130-460 years (depending on the LXX manuscript: Vaticanus has 130 + 330) to the post-flood chronology. The insertion is confirmed at Luke 3:36, where Luke's genealogy of Jesus includes "the son of Cainan, the son of Arphaxad." Luke demonstrably follows the LXX here, not the MT.

**The LXX age differences for Genesis 11 (MT vs. LXX, Vaticanus):**

| Patriarch | MT: Age at 1st son | LXX: Age at 1st son | MT: Remaining years | LXX: Remaining years |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Shem | 100 | 100 | 500 | 500 |
| Arphaxad | 35 | 135 | 403 | 430 |
| [Cainan] | — | 130 | — | 330 |
| Shelah | 30 | 130 | 403 | 330 |
| Eber | 34 | 134 | 430 | 370 |
| Peleg | 30 | 130 | 209 | 209 |
| Reu | 32 | 132 | 207 | 207 |
| Serug | 30 | 130 | 200 | 200 |
| Nahor | 29 | 79 | 119 | 129 |
| Terah | 70 | 70 | — (death at 205) | — (death at 205) |

The LXX consistently adds 100 years to the age at first son for most patriarchs (except Shem, Nahor, and Terah). The effect is to significantly lengthen the period between the flood and the birth of Abraham — by approximately 650-900 years depending on how the Cainan entry is handled. The MT places the birth of Abraham approximately 290 years after the flood; the LXX places it approximately 1,070-1,200 years after the flood.

**The chronological significance:** This difference has enormous implications for biblical chronology and was central to debates about the date of creation. Archbishop Ussher's famous 4004 BCE date follows the MT. Those following the LXX (including Eusebius and early church chronologers) arrived at much earlier dates (typically 5500 BCE or earlier). The LXX chronology creates a much longer primeval history that more comfortably accommodates ancient Near Eastern civilization as known from archaeology.

**EMET translation policy:** The EMET follows the MT (Leningrad Codex) in all numbers and omits the Cainan generation entirely, as the primary source is the Leningrad Codex. The LXX variants are documented fully in these notes. The Samaritan Pentateuch provides a third set of numbers (generally intermediate between MT and LXX for the pre-flood genealogy, but with different patterns in the post-flood genealogy). For Genesis 11, the SP agrees with the MT in omitting Cainan and generally follows the MT ages.

---

### On 11:26 — Terah Fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran: A Birth-Order Question

**וַיְחִי-תֶרַח שִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת-אַבְרָם אֶת-נָחוֹר וְאֶת-הָרָן:** Terah was seventy years old when he fathered "Abram, Nahor, and Haran." This does not mean all three were born in Terah's seventieth year — it means Terah began the process of fathering these three sons at age seventy. The eldest of the three is almost certainly Haran (who dies before his father — a significant detail in v. 28), and Abram is likely the youngest or middle son, despite being listed first (as with Shem being listed first among Noah's sons though not necessarily the eldest). The listing of Abram first signals his narrative primacy, not his birth order.

This creates a chronological tension: if Terah was 70 when he began fathering and Abram was born when Terah was 130 (since Acts 7:4 says Abram left Haran after Terah's death at 205, and Abram was 75 when he left Haran per 12:4 — 205 − 75 = 130), then Abram was born when Terah was 130, not 70. This suggests Haran (the firstborn, who died young) was born when Terah was 70, and Abram was born 60 years later. The formula "Terah lived 70 years and fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran" is thus a collective statement about the beginning of Terah's fathering, not a claim that Abram was born at Terah's age 70.

---

### On 11:27 — אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת תֶּרַח: The Sixth Toledot Formula

The sixth toledot formula (2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 10:1; 11:10; 11:27) introduces the narrative section that will occupy the remainder of Genesis (chapters 12-50): the stories of the patriarchs — Abram/Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph. The Terah toledot is the hinge between primeval history (Genesis 1-11) and patriarchal history (Genesis 12-50). It opens at the very end of chapter 11 precisely to signal that what follows (the Abram narrative from Genesis 12 onward) belongs to the toledot of Terah — Abram's story is narrated within the framework of his father's family account.

---

### On 11:28 — אוּר כַּשְׂדִּים: Ur of the Chaldeans

**בְּאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים (be'ur Kasdim, "in Ur of the Chaldeans"):** This is one of the most discussed geographical references in Genesis. The identification of Ur:

1. **Southern Mesopotamia (classical identification):** The great Sumerian city of Ur (modern Tell el-Muqayyar in southern Iraq), excavated by Leonard Woolley (1922-34), who famously identified it as Abraham's birthplace. Woolley's "Royal Cemetery of Ur" revealed a sophisticated urban civilization of the 3rd millennium BCE. Ur was a major center of the moon-god Nanna/Sin worship. This remains the dominant identification.

2. **Northern Mesopotamia/Haran region (alternative):** Some scholars (Cyrus Gordon, others) have argued for an "Ur" in the vicinity of Haran in northern Mesopotamia (modern southern Turkey), noting that the family ultimately resettles in Haran and that "Chaldeans" (כַּשְׂדִּים, Kasdim) is typically associated in the Bible with Babylonia, which would support the southern identification.

The qualifier "of the Chaldeans" (כַּשְׂדִּים) is itself chronologically interesting: the Chaldeans (Kaldu) were a late second/early first millennium BCE Aramean-related tribal confederacy that settled in southern Babylonia and eventually produced the Neo-Babylonian dynasty (Nebuchadnezzar II). Their designation of the ancient city of Ur as "Ur of the Chaldeans" reflects the first millennium perspective of the narrator — "Chaldeans" is a later ethnic label applied retrospectively to the region. This is consistent with the view that the Genesis narratives received their final literary form during or after the period of Babylonian hegemony.

**עַל-פְּנֵי אָבִיו תֶּרַח (al-penei aviv Terach, "upon the face of his father Terah"):** The phrase "upon the face of" means "in the presence of" or "before" — Haran died while his father was still alive. To die before one's father was in the ancient world a profound tragedy and reversal of natural order — and it sets up the reader for the unexpected: Terah's family line faces extinction from within. This poignant notice prepares the great theme of Genesis 12-22: the promise of seed to Abram and Sarai, who are barren (v. 30), against all expectation.

---

### On 11:29-30 — Sarai the Barren: The Narrative Setup for Genesis 12-22

**וְשֵׁם אֵשֶׁת-אַבְרָם שָׂרָי (veshem eshet-Avram Sarai, "and the name of Abram's wife was Sarai"):** The name שָׂרָי (Sarai) means "my princess" or "my noble lady" — a form of שַׂר (sar, "prince/ruler/noble"). It is changed to שָׂרָה (Sarah, "princess/noblewoman") in Genesis 17:15 at the covenant of circumcision, simultaneously with the change of אַבְרָם (Abram, "exalted father") to אַבְרָהָם (Abraham, "father of a multitude"). Both name changes signal the covenant transformation.

**מִלְכָּה (Milcah):** Milcah ("queen") is the daughter of Haran and the wife of Nahor. She will be the grandmother of Rebekah (22:20-23), who will become Isaac's wife. The genealogical web is already being woven.

**יִסְכָּה (Iscah):** Mentioned only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. Her identity is unknown. Ancient tradition (Targum Pseudo-Jonathan; Josephus) identified Iscah with Sarai/Sarah — i.e., that Sarai was also the daughter of Haran and therefore Abram's niece (making the marriage an endogamous or close-kin union). This identification is speculative but ancient. The narrator's inclusion of this otherwise irrelevant name suggests she had a role in tradition that is not preserved in the canonical text.

**וַתְּהִי שָׂרַי עֲקָרָה אֵין לָהּ וָלָד (vattehi Sarai aqarah ein lah walad, "And Sarai was barren — she had no child"):** This is the most important single verse in the Terah genealogy. All that follows — the entire Abram/Abraham narrative of Genesis 12-22, the covenant, the promise of land and seed, the birth of Isaac, the binding of Isaac — turns on this impossibility. God calls a barren woman and a husband who has no heir to become the parents of a great nation. The explicit notice of Sarai's barrenness at the end of the primeval history is the dark cloud from which the light of the patriarchal promise will emerge.

The double notation is unusual: "barren... she had no child." Typically one qualifier would suffice. The redundancy is emphatic — the narrator wants the reader to sit with the impossibility. אֵין לָהּ וָלָד (ein lah walad) — "there is no child to her" — the word לָהּ ("to her") is a possessive of relationship: the child is hers, and there is none. This is not merely a biological fact but a statement of relational deprivation.

---

### On 11:31 — From Ur to Haran: The Interrupted Journey

**לָלֶכֶת אַרְצָה כְּנָעַן (lalekhet artsah Kenaan, "to go to the land of Canaan"):** The destination is Canaan — yet they only reach Haran (modern Harran in southeastern Turkey, on the upper Euphrates, a major crossroads city of the ancient Near East) and stop there. The journey is incomplete; Terah settles in Haran and dies there. It is left to Abram to complete the journey to Canaan — which he does in Genesis 12:5 (וַיֵּצְאוּ לָלֶכֶת אַרְצָה כְּנָעַן, "and they went forth to go to the land of Canaan"). The exact same phrase is used in both passages (11:31 and 12:5) — a deliberate literary echo: what Terah set out to do and could not complete, Abram does.

**Why does Terah stop in Haran?** The text does not explain. Haran (the city) has the same name as Terah's dead son Haran — whether this is coincidence, folk etymology, or commemoration of the son's memory at a city he was associated with is unknowable from the text. Haran (Harran) was a major center of moon-god (Sin) worship — the same deity worshipped at Ur. Joshua 24:2 explicitly states that Terah "served other gods." The journey from Ur to Haran may be a journey between two moon-god cult cities, with Canaan — the land of YHWH's promise — as the intended but not-yet-reached destination.

---

### On 11:32 — Terah's Death and the End of the Primeval History

**וַיִּהְיוּ יְמֵי-תֶרַח חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים וּמָאתַיִם שָׁנָה וַיָּמָת תֶּרַח בְּחָרָן:** "And the days of Terah were two hundred and five years and Terah died in Haran."

This verse closes the primeval history of Genesis (chapters 1-11). The death of Terah in Haran is the narrative hinge: only after Terah's death (or simultaneously with it — cf. the chronological puzzle of 11:26 and 12:4 discussed above) does YHWH call Abram in chapter 12. The primeval history ends not with a bang but with a quiet death notice — an old man in a foreign city, his son dead before him, his daughter-in-law barren, his journey unfinished.

**LXX 11:32:** The LXX has the same total (205 years) for Terah. The SP reads 145 years for Terah's age at death — a variant that resolves the chronological tension noted above (making Terah die while Abram was still in Haran, rather than after Abram left, as Stephen's speech in Acts 7:4 implies by following the MT's 205 years). The 145-year SP reading would make Terah 75 when Abram was born and dead by the time Abram left for Canaan at 75 — fitting Stephen's account perfectly. But the MT and LXX agree at 205, and the EMET follows the Leningrad Codex.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 11

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 11:1 | דְּבָרִים אֲחָדִים ("one words") | φωνὴ μία ("one voice") + πᾶσιν ("for all") | LXX smooths difficult MT; adds "for all" |
| 11:9 | name called בָּבֶל | name called Σύγχυσις (Confusion) | LXX translates rather than transliterates Babel |
| 11:10 | Shem: 100 years, fathered Arphaxad | Σημ: 100 years, fathered Αρφαξαδ | LXX agrees with MT at v. 10 |
| 11:12 | Arphaxad (35) fathered Shelah | Arphaxad (135) fathered Cainan | LXX: +100 years; inserts Cainan generation |
| 11:12-13 | [no Cainan entry] | Cainan: 130 + 330 years | Major variant: LXX adds full Cainan generation; cf. Luke 3:36 |
| 11:14 | Shelah (30) fathered Eber | Shelah (130) fathered Eber | LXX: +100 years |
| 11:16 | Eber (34) fathered Peleg | Eber (134) fathered Peleg | LXX: +100 years |
| 11:18 | Peleg (30) fathered Reu | Peleg (130) fathered Reu | LXX: +100 years |
| 11:20 | Reu (32) fathered Serug | Reu (132) fathered Serug | LXX: +100 years |
| 11:22 | Serug (30) fathered Nahor | Serug (130) fathered Nahor | LXX: +100 years |
| 11:24 | Nahor (29) fathered Terah | Nahor (79) fathered Terah | LXX: +50 years |
| 11:32 | Terah died aged 205 | Terah died aged 205 | LXX agrees; SP reads 145 (significant variant) |

---

## Dead Sea Scrolls, Samaritan Pentateuch, and Versional Notes

**Dead Sea Scrolls:** No significant Genesis 11 fragments are extant from Qumran. The Genesis Apocryphon (1QapGen) from Qumran does not cover chapter 11. The Book of Jubilees (found in multiple copies at Qumran) retells the Shem genealogy without the Cainan insertion, supporting the antiquity of the MT tradition.

**Samaritan Pentateuch:** The SP agrees with the MT in omitting Cainan. For the patriarchal ages, the SP follows a pattern similar to the LXX for pre-flood patriarchs (adding 100 years to the age at first son) but the post-flood numbers show complex divergences. For Terah specifically, the SP reads 145 years at death (vs. MT/LXX 205), resolving the chronological tension with Acts 7:4. The EMET follows the MT in all numbers.

**Targum Onkelos:** Renders the Babel narrative straightforwardly, without major expansions. Onkelos renders שָׂפָה ("lip") as לִישָׁנָא ("tongue/language") throughout — normalizing the unusual body-part imagery of the MT.

**Targum Pseudo-Jonathan:** Adds extensive legendary material: identifies the builders with Nimrod and his followers, describes the three-part purpose of the tower (one third as a place of worship for idols, one third for attack on heaven with weapons, one third as a signal fire), and identifies specific punishments for the builders (some turned to apes, some scattered, some confused in language).
